coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent and smartphone-assisted colorimetric dual-mode sensor for Ag+ detection based on orange-red emitting carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Jia, Jing
Wang, Hongliang
Quan, Ruxia
Shuang, Shaomin
Wu, Zhifang
Li, Sijin
coredata.dc:description: A novel fluorescent and smartphone-assisted colorimetric dual-mode sensor is constructed for Ag+ monitoring based on orange-red emitting carbon dots (or-CDs). The as-synthesized or-CDs possess strict excitation independence and show specific response to Ag+. Ag+ can induce fluorescence quenching of the or-CDs through dynamic quenching characteristic. The quenching efficiency and Ag+ concentration show good linear relationships in the ranges of 0.8–10 μM and 10–100 μM with the limit of detection (LOD) down to 56 nM. More interestingly, these phenomena are accompanied by visible color conversion (red-brown) under the sunlight. So, we develop a portable smartphone-assisted colorimetry for Ag+ monitoring via the red-green-blue (RGB) analysis with the linear range of 10–80 μM and LOD of 0.78 μM. Furthermore, the or-CDs have realized the monitor of Ag+ in human serum and the Fenhe River with good recoveries (96.37–103.20%) and relative standard deviations (RSDs, <3%) by fluorescence and smartphone-based RGB analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Orange-red fluorescence
Colorimetry
Smartphone
Ag+

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Ag+ in real samples
Detection of Ag+ in real sample

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 82027804 , 81971655 , U22A6008 and 21874087 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image and (b) particle size distributions of the or-CDs.
(a) FTIR and (b) XPS spectra of the or-CDs.
High resolution XPS spectra of (a) C 1s, (b) N 1s, (c) O 1s and (d) S 2p of the or-CDs.
(a) Absorbance, excitation and emission spectra of the or-CDs. (b) Excitation-dependent emission spectra of the or-CDs.
FL intensity of the or-CDs in the presence of different metal ions (100 μΜ of Ag+ and 500 μM of others) and the Ag++Mn + mixture solutions.
(a) FL spectra of the or-CDs upon adding different concentrations of Ag+; (b) The linear relationship between (F0–F) and Ag+ concentrations.

(a) The photograph of sensor system in the presence of different concentrations of Ag+. (b) The linear relationship between (R/(G + B)) and Ag+ concentrations. (c) The RGB values of the or-CDs mixed with Ag+ of different concentrations.
Illustration of the synthetic procedure of the or-CDs and their application in detection of Ag+ in the fluorescent and smartphone-assisted colorimetric dual-mode manner.
Comparison of the detection performance of various method to Ag+.
Determination of Ag+ in real samples by the or-CDs (n = 6).

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: /: The serum sample itself has a light-yellow color, which will interfere with the results of the smartphone-assisted colorimetry.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent and smartphone-assisted colorimetric dual-mode sensor for Ag+ detection based on orange-red emitting carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Nuclear Medicine, The First Hospital of Shanxi Medical University, and Collaborative Innovation Center of Molecular Imaging Precision Medical, Shanxi Medical University, Taiyuan, 030001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The First Hospital of Shanxi Medical University, and Collaborative Innovation Center of Molecular Imaging Precision Medical

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Nuclear Medicine, The First Hospital of Shanxi Medical University, and Collaborative Innovation Center of Molecular Imaging Precision Medical, Shanxi Medical University, Taiyuan, 030001, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan, 030006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan, 030006, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel fluorescent and smartphone-assisted colorimetric dual-mode sensor is constructed for Ag+ monitoring based on orange-red emitting carbon dots (or-CDs). The as-synthesized or-CDs possess strict excitation independence and show specific response to Ag+. Ag+ can induce fluorescence quenching of the or-CDs through dynamic quenching characteristic. The quenching efficiency and Ag+ concentration show good linear relationships in the ranges of 0.8–10 μM and 10–100 μM with the limit of detection (LOD) down to 56 nM. More interestingly, these phenomena are accompanied by visible color conversion (red-brown) under the sunlight. So, we develop a portable smartphone-assisted colorimetry for Ag+ monitoring via the red-green-blue (RGB) analysis with the linear range of 10–80 μM and LOD of 0.78 μM. Furthermore, the or-CDs have realized the monitor of Ag+ in human serum and the Fenhe River with good recoveries (96.37–103.20%) and relative standard deviations (RSDs, <3%) by fluorescence and smartphone-based RGB analysis.

Scheme 1. Illustration of the synthetic procedure of the or-CDs and their application in detection of Ag+ in the fluorescent and smartphone-assisted colorimetric dual-mode manner.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Ag+ is one of the trace elements in human tissue, which has the function of disinfection and sterilization [1]. It can strongly attract the sulfhydryl group on the protease in the bacterial body, rapidly bind to it and make the protease lose activity, resulting in bacterial death. The World Health Organization stipulates that the concentration of Ag+ in drinking water should not be higher than 0.5 μM [2]. Excessive Ag+ in drinking water will interact with amino acids or metabolites and damage the human cells, causing a variety of diseases such as nervous system damage, heart enlargement, skin damage, etc [3]. Common methods for Ag+ detection include mass spectrometry [4], atomic absorption spectrometry [5] and electrochemistry [6], which have satisfactory sensitivity. Nevertheless, the above methods still suffer from some drawbacks with specialized instrumentation, complicated operation and time consuming, remaining difficulties in on-site and real-time monitoring.

Fluorescent assay exhibits more advantages, such as high sensitivity, simple operation and real-time detection [7–9]. Based on these merits, numerous fluorescent probes have been constructed for Ag+ detection, including organic probe [10], semiconductor quantum dots [11], noble-metal clusters [12] and carbon dots (CDs) [13]. Among them, CDs have remarkable photoluminescence, excellent photostability, low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility. These advantages make them stand out from other fluorescent probes [14–16]. Massive efforts have been made to use CDs to detect Ag+ [17–20]. For example, Li et al. [21] found that green fluorescence G-CD can be selectively quenched by Ag+. Compared with the single fluorescence response, the combination of fluorescence method and colorimetry can make the detection process both highly sensitive and visual detection function, and improve the accuracy and convenience of analytical strategy [22]. Lu et al. [23] developed a fluorescent and colorimetric dual-mode sensing platform for ultra-sensitive detection of Ag+ with the LOD down to 37 nM. In recent years, colorimetric sensing systems mediated by smartphones have become a research hotspot. Smartphones have high-resolution cameras and powerful computing power, which allow them to capture color changes with high sensitivity and quickly read through red-green-blue (RGB) color images [24]. Smartphone-assisted colorimetry has the significant advantage of being easy to use, portable and on-situ detection. Tang et al. [25] used N-CDs as fluorescent probes combined with smartphones to achieve direct, visual and on-site monitoring of Ag+ in lake water. However, the emission wavelengths of these CDs are limited in blue to yellow light region. The short-wave emitting has shallow tissue penetration, cannot avoid the interference of spontaneous fluorescence, and has certain phototoxicity in bioimaging and biosensing [26]. To avoid these problems, it is of significance to prepare long-wave emitting CDs for the detection of Ag+.

The carbon source containing aromatic ring is easy to condense into large conjugated structure in the process of heating carbonization, which reduces the energy band gap and increases the probability of long-wave emission [27]. In addition, heteroatom doping (such as N, S and P) can regulate the energy gap and electronic structure of CDs, thus improving the fluorescence quantum yield (QY) and changing emission wavelength [28]. Therefore, the compounds containing conjugated structure and heteroatom are considered to be promising precursors for the synthesis of long-wave emitting CDs.

Herein, we select p-aminobenzenesulfonic acid and p-phenylenediamine as precursors to synthesize orange-red emitting CDs (or-CDs) (Scheme 1 ). The resulting or-CDs possess strict excitation independence and excellent photostability. Ag+ can induce fluorescence dynamic quenching of the or-CDs. Meanwhile, these phenomena are accompanied by visible color conversion (red-brown). So, we develop a portable smartphone-assisted colorimetry for Ag+ monitoring. Moreover, the or-CDs have realized the monitor of Ag+ in human serum and the Fenhe River with good recoveries and RSDs by fluorescence and smartphone-based RGB analysis.

Sulfanilic acid, p-phenylendiamine (PPD) and AgNO3 are all purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. Human serum is provided by the Shanxi University Hospital. The water samples come from the Fenhe River in Taiyuan City, Shanxi Province. All chemical reagents reach the analytical grade and can be used directly without further purification. Deionized water (≥18.25 MU·cm) is used all the experiment procedure.

The or-CDs were synthesized through hydrothermal method using sulfanilic acid and PPD. Briefly, 240 mg of sulfanilic acid and 150 mg of PPD were first dissolved into 10 mL of ultrapure water with ultrasonic treatment. Then, the uniform solution was heated at 220 °C for 7 h in the stainless-steel autoclaves lined with Teflon. After cooling to room temperature, the crude product was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to discard precipitate. A 0.22 μm filter membrane was employed to eliminate the impurities in the solution. Finally, powder of the or-CDs was obtained by freeze drying.

In a typical run, 200 μg of the or-CDs were dispersed into 1980 μL of phosphate buffer solution (PBS, 0.01 M, pH 7.4). Then, 20 μL Ag+ with various concentrations (0.01–20 mM) were added the above solution, respectively. The obtained solution was incubated for 1 min at room temperature. Then, the corresponding fluorescence data were collected at the emission of 606 nm. The selectivity experiments of the or-CDs were performed by adding 500 μM other metal ions to replace 100 μM Ag+ in similar a way as described above.

For smartphone-based Ag+ detection, 20 μL Ag+ with various concentrations (0.01–10 mM) were mixed with 1980 μL the or-CDs’ (200 μg) PBS solution into the centrifuge tube, respectively. After equilibration for 3 min, the colorimetric signal of the detection zone was measured by using ColorDesk software (Ver 1.2.5) and ColorPiker (Ver 3.1) installed on a smartphone which was fixed at 10 cm distance from the detection zone. The RGB color mode was chosen in current study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Ag+ in real samples

ce:section.ce:para: Human blood serum was collected from healthy adult volunteer in the Shanxi University Hospital. In brief, 3 mL trichloroacetic acid (quality fraction 15%) was added to 1 mL serum to inactivate and precipitate proteins. After vigorous shaking for 15 min, the mixture was centrifuge at 9000 rpm for 15 min. The supernatant was collected and modulated neutral through sodium hydroxide (NaOH). The resulting samples were diluted 100 times with deionized water. Finally, the solution was detected by fluorescence analysis.

River water comes from the Fenhe River (Taiyuan City, Shanxi Province). The sediment was removed by 0.22 μm membrane filtration. Different concentrations of Ag+ were spiked into the water samples. Then, the resulting uniform solutions were detected by fluorescence and smartphone-based RGB analysis.

We use transmission electron microscopy (TEM) to characterize the morphology and size of the or-CDs. The or-CDs are spherical and evenly dispersed with average diameters of 4.88 nm (Fig. 1 ). Then, the surface compositions are characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As shown in Fig. 2 a, the peak centered at 3400 cm−1 is assigned to the O–H/N–H bond. The peaks at 2950 cm−1 and 2600 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of C–H and S–H, respectively. A peak at 1655 cm−1 is due to CO stretching vibrations. Strong absorption bands at 1456 cm−1 and 1160 cm−1 indicate the presence of C–N and C–O/SO, respectively. The tiny band at 1360 cm−1 is due to CC vibrations. The full range XPS spectra show four obvious peaks of C1s (285.7 eV), N1s (401.3 eV), O1s (528.5 eV) and S2p (169.1 eV) (Fig. 2b). As shown in Fig. 3 a, the C1s exhibit three main peaks, which correspond to C–C/CC (284.9 eV), C–N/C–O/C–S (258.7 eV) and CO (286.5 eV), respectively. The high-resolved N1s spectra at 399.4 eV, 401.2 eV and 401.9 eV are ascribed to pyridinic N, amino N and pyrrolic N, respectively (Fig. 3b). The O1s can be divided into three peaks, corresponding to S–O/SO (531.1 eV), C–O (531.7 eV) and CO (533.0 eV), respectively (Fig. 3c). The S2p peaks at 168.3 eV, 169.1 eV and 169.9 eV indicate the presence of S2p3/2, S2p1/2 and S–O/SO, respectively (Fig. 3d). As a result, we deduce that there are abundant functional groups on the surface of the or-CDs such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino.

The remarkable optical behaviors of the or-CDs are performed by UV–vis absorption and FL spectra. Fig. 4 a show that two distinct absorption peaks appear at 271 nm and 519 nm, corresponding to the π-π* transition of the sp2 aromatic [29] and the n-π* transition of the surface-attached functional groups [30], respectively. The maximum excitation and emission peaks of the or-CDs are located at 540 nm and 606 nm, accompanied by bright orange-red fluorescence. As displayed in Fig. 4b, the or-CDs exhibit excitation-independent characteristic, indicating the presence of concentrated size distribution and uniform emission state. Using Rhodamine B (QY = 89% in ethanol) as a reference, the relative QY of the or-CDs is calculated to be 18.4%. As shown in Figure S1, the or-CDs have excellent photostability under different conditions (such as pH, ion, light irradiation and storage time).

The or-CDs are attached with multiple functional groups on their surface, which can combine with specific molecules or ions to achieve the analysis of target substances. The selectivity and anti-interference experiments are performed by adding some metal ions to the or-CDs’ solution. As displayed in Fig. 5 , Ag+ can quench the fluorescence of or-CDs significantly (F/F0 = 0.20), while other metal ions have little or negligible effect, indicating the or-CDs have high selectivity towards Ag+. And only Ag+ can cause significant color change in the or-CDs’ solutions (Figure S3). When adding these potentially interfering ions into the Ag+ quenched or-CDs solutions, negligible influences on the quenched fluorescence are observed, indicating the excellent anti-interference ability of the or-CDs for Ag+ detection.

As the increase of Ag+ concentration, the fluorescence intensities of the or-CDs decrease with different degree (Fig. 6 a). As shown in Fig. 6b, the quenching gap (F0–F) decreases rapidly in the concentration range of 0.8–10 μM and then decreases slowly at high concentrations of 10–100 μM. We speculate that the quenching effect is primarily ascribed to the electron transfer between the electron acceptor of Ag+ and electron donor of the or-CDs. Ag+ can interact with –COO/−OH/−SH on the or-CDs surface, which facilitates electron transfer from the excited state of the or-CDs to the unoccupied orbital of Ag+ and results in fluorescence quenching. When Ag+ concentration is lower than 10 μM, abundant reaction sites are more likely to promote energy transfer, so the quenching efficiency is faster. At high concentration, Ag+ have to first diffuse to the surface of the or-CDs and then interact with limited binding sites, so the quenching efficiency decreases. The corresponding LOD is calculated to be 56 nM based on 3σ/S, where σ is the standard deviation of the lowest signal (n = 20) and S is the slope of the linear calibration plot. We further evaluate the accuracy of this linear ranges. As shown in Table S1, the interference of small volume change on the fluorescence intensity is eliminated by setting PBS solution as the control group. And we also find the fluorescence does not change when the concentration of Ag+ is greater than 100 μM (Tabel S2). As a result, the linear ranges of Ag+ determined by the fluorescence model is accurate.

As shown in Table 1 , previously reported atomic absorption spectrometry [5] and electrochemical method [6] show high sensitivity. The or-CDs are slightly inferior in sensitivity, but they show more advantages in easy operation, real-time and on-situ detection. Compared with the single fluorescence response, the combination of fluorescence method and colorimetry can make the detection process simpler and more intuitive. And the linear ranges and LODs of the or-CDs are better than them [18–20]. When compared to other CDs [23,25], almost all of them could achieve fluorescence and colorimetric dual-mode detection, but the or-CDs can emit orange-red fluorescence, which can avoid spontaneous fluorescence and possess stronger tissue penetration, having potential in biological applications.

We further explore the mechanism for Ag+ recognition by the or-CDs. As shown in Figure S2 and Table S3, the fluorescence decay curves and lifetime values of the or-CDs is 3.25 ns and the or-CDs + Ag+ is 2.59 ns. The inconformity of PL lifetimes indicate that dynamic quenching has occurred. We further speculate that there is an electron transfer between the or-CDs and Ag+ leading to fluorescence quenching.

With increase of Ag+ concentration, the solutions of the or-CDs vary from red to brown (Fig. 7 a and Fig. 4a). The ColorDesk application installed on my smartphone can capture the color changes of different solutions and give corresponding RGB values (Fig. 7c and Fig. 4c). As shown in Figure S4b, when the concentration of Ag+ is too low (0–10 μM), the color change is not obvious and there is no linear relationship between R/(G + B) and the concentration of Ag+. When the concentration is 10–80 μM, there is a good linear relationship, along with a LOD of 0.78 μM (LOD = 3σ/S). In order to verify the accuracy of this method, we compare the RGB values of ColorDesk software with the ColorPiker software. As shown in Figure S5, the ColorPiker application installed on my smartphone can capture the color changes of different solutions and give corresponding RGB values. The measured R/(G + B) values are listed in Table S4. We compare the results of the two methods using linear regression analysis (Figure S6). The relationship between the above two methods satisfies the following linear equation:
(1) yColorPiker = 1.085xColorDesk-0.1332 (R2 = 0.997)
where x is the results of ColorDesk and y refers the results of ColorPiker. The fitting results show that the two methods have good consistency in the range of 10–80 μM. As a result, smartphone-assisted colorimetry is considered to have significant potential for convenient, on-situ and rapid detection of Ag+. However, the sensitivity of this smartphone-based colorimetry needs to be further improved.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Ag+ in real sample
ce:section.ce:para: In order to verify the practicability of the or-CD-based probes, the contents of Ag+ in human serum and the Fenhe River are determined by the standard addition method. Ag+ with the concentrations of 10 μM, 30 μM and 50 μM are dispersed into the real samples, respectively. The measured recoveries are 96.37–103.20% and the RSDs are less than 3% (Table 2 ) by the fluorescent method and smartphone-assisted colorimetry. In actual samples, multiple metal ions may be present simultaneously. We mixed 14 different metal ions (Ag+, Hg2+, Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Mn2+, Li+, Pb+, Co2+) together in the or-CDs’ PBS solution to mimic the complexity of the actual sample. As shown in Figure S7, the quenching efficiency and RGB values of Ag+ in coexisting solution is basically the same as the original quenching efficiency. This indicates that the or-CDs is stable in complex sample detection where multiple metals coexist.

In addition, we studied the stability of the or-CDs’ sensing performance. As shown in Table S5, the or-CDs stored for 2.5 months were used as dual-mode probes for Ag+ detection. The added Ag+ concentrations are known to be 6 μΜ and 60 μΜ, respectively. The corresponding quenching values (F0–F) and R/(G + B) values are calculated according to the fluorescence signals and RGB data. By substituting the (F0–F) and the R/(G + B) values into the corresponding linear equation, the concentration of Ag+ can be calculated. The consistency between the real values and the calculated values of Ag+ indicates the sensing performance of the or-CDs is stability.

Here, the fluorescent and smartphone-assisted colorimetric dual-mode sensor based on the or-CDs is constructed for convenient, on-situ and rapid detection of Ag+. The or-CDs have strict excitation independence and excellent photostability. The or-CDs can response to Ag+ with high selectivity and sensitivity, and the fluorescence are quenched based on dynamic quenching characteristic. Meanwhile, the smartphone-assisted colorimetry can quickly and easily capture the color change of the solution and give the specific RGB values. Furthermore, the or-CDs have realized the monitor of Ag+ in human serum and the Fenhe River by fluorescence and smartphone-based RGB analysis. It can be concluded that this dual-mode sensor is a promising portable detection platform for Ag+ with high sensitivity, real time and visibility.

Jing Jia: Formal analysis, Resources, Writing – original draft, preparation, Writing – review & editing. Hongliang Wang: Project administration, Supervision. Ruxia Quan: Investigation. Shaomin Shuang: Supervision. Zhifang Wu: Project administration, Supervision. Sijin Li: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declared that they have no any commercial or associative interest that represents a conflict of interest in connection with the work.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 82027804, 81971655, U22A6008 and 21874087).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mtchem.2023.101730.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Role of soil and foliar-applied carbon dots in plant iron biofortification and cadmium mitigation by triggering opposite iron signaling in roots
